coredata.dc:title: Facet-dependent photocatalytic and photoelectric properties of CQDs/TiO2 composites under visible irradiation

Zhao, Wei

Yao, Conghui
He, Yulu
Ran, Xia
Guo, Lijun
Li, Tianfeng
coredata.dc:description: The construction of photocatalytic heterostructures and the study of carrier kinetics are significant for developing novel functional materials and understanding catalytic mechanisms. Here, we synthesized the heterostructural composites of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles with (101) or (001) as the main exposed facets by hydrothermal method, named CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001). The facet-dependent sensitization and photocatalysis properties of these composites have been investigated using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. The results indicate that the electron transfer process between CQDs and TiO2 is associated with the exposed facet of TiO2. The electron transfer rate from CQDs to TiO2(101) is two orders of magnitude larger than that to TiO2(001). The CQDs/TiO2(101) composite also demonstrates three times higher photoelectric conversion than CQDs/TiO2(001). The facet-dependent catalytic performances of CQDs/TiO2 composites have been evaluated by the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The rate constant of CQDs/TiO2(101) for the degradation of intermediates (Rh-110) shows 1.82 times higher than that of CQDs/TiO2(001). These findings indicate the effective loading of CQDs on TiO2(101) nanoparticles can be expected to efficiently improve the photoelectric and photocatalytic properties of TiO2-based heterostructure under visible irradiation, and are significant for deep understanding the facet-dependent photocatalytic mechanism and property.

head.ce:title: Facet-dependent photocatalytic and photoelectric properties of CQDs/TiO2 composites under visible irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, Henan University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaojuan Wang and Wei Zhao contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of photocatalytic heterostructures and the study of carrier kinetics are significant for developing novel functional materials and understanding catalytic mechanisms. Here, we synthesized the heterostructural composites of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and TiO2 nanoparticles with (101) or (001) as the main exposed facets by hydrothermal method, named CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001). The facet-dependent sensitization and photocatalysis properties of these composites have been investigated using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. The results indicate that the electron transfer process between CQDs and TiO2 is associated with the exposed facet of TiO2. The electron transfer rate from CQDs to TiO2(101) is two orders of magnitude larger than that to TiO2(001). The CQDs/TiO2(101) composite also demonstrates three times higher photoelectric conversion than CQDs/TiO2(001). The facet-dependent catalytic performances of CQDs/TiO2 composites have been evaluated by the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The rate constant of CQDs/TiO2(101) for the degradation of intermediates (Rh-110) shows 1.82 times higher than that of CQDs/TiO2(001). These findings indicate the effective loading of CQDs on TiO2(101) nanoparticles can be expected to efficiently improve the photoelectric and photocatalytic properties of TiO2-based heterostructure under visible irradiation, and are significant for deep understanding the facet-dependent photocatalytic mechanism and property.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of population and the deepening of industrialization, energy crisis and environmental pollution have become urgent problems. Photocatalytic materials and technologies have been demonstrated the promising candidates to address these issues because they can environmental-friendly convert solar energy into clean hydrogen energy and effectively degrade the organic pollutants to non-toxic small molecules [1–4]. As a representative catalytic material, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been widely investigated and extensively used in the photocatalytic technology due to its high activity, great chemical stability, non-toxicity, low cost, etc. [5,6]. However, for anatase TiO2 with the bandgap energy of 3.2 eV, it can only be excited by ultraviolet light with a wavelength less than 387 nm, resulting in a low photocatalytic efficiency under sunlight illumination [7,8]. In addition, the higher recombination rate of photo-generated electron hole pair of TiO2 also leads to a decrease in catalytic efficiency [9,10]. Therefore, challenges still remain in the construction of TiO2-based composites to improve their photocatalytic properties for practical applications.

In the past few decades, a lot of strategies have been studied to increase the lifetime of photogenerated electron hole pairs and reduce the bandgap of TiO2-based materials. For example, doping metals and non-metals such as gold, aluminum, nitrogen, sulfur and chlorine into the TiO2 lattice to reduce the bandgap and improve its response to visible light [11–14]. The separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers can likewise be improved by depositing plasma metal nanoparticles, combining with other semiconductors, or surface sensitizing with organic dyes [15–18]. In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) have spurred interests due to their suitable bandgap, large specific surface area and rich surface properties. Many types of narrow bandgap QDs have been used as visible light absorbers of TiO2 nanoparticles [19–21]. The composites composed of TiO2 and QDs have been increasingly investigated as effective photocatalysts for organic dye degradation, hydrogen evolution or quantum dot-sensitized solar cells. Although many achievements have been made, some additional disadvantages could also be introduced, such as poor chemical stability, light corrosion resistance of the photocatalysts, heavy metal toxicity of cadmium- or lead-based QDs and so on.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of green material with low cost, non-toxicity, excellent photoelectric properties and superior stability against photobleaching and easy functionalized modification. CQDs have likewise been demonstrated with broad full solar spectrum absorption, which enables the sunlight in visible and near-infrared regions to be effectively utilized. Therefore, in addition to being widely used in biomedicine, photodetectors, photosensors and solar cells, CQDs can also be employed as the sensitizing materials to improve the photocatalytic efficiency of wide-bandgap photocatalysts [22–25]. CQDs and TiO2 composites can inherit the advantages of the two materials and even generate new photochemical characteristics. The composites constituted by these two moieties can be expected to facilitate interfacial electron transfer and the separation of photo-generated carriers, thus greatly enhancing the catalytic efficiency.

So far, numerous reports of CQDs-TiO2 composites have been developed in the study of photodegradation of organic dyes and photolysis of water for hydrogen production [26–28]. Due to the complexity of heterogeneous catalysis and underling mechanisms, photocatalytic performances and light energy conversions can be often affected by varieties of factors, such as preparation method, morphology and size of materials, specific surface area, crystalline phase and defects of particles and so on [29–31]. These factors usually show different influences on the photo-generated charges separation, the electron transfer rate and the free radicals production for different TiO2-based composites. For anatase TiO2, besides the above factors, the activity of exposed facets also demonstrates significant effects on the efficiency of photocatalysts and photoelectric conversion [32,33]. Therefore, the combination of CQDs with TiO2 with different facets makes the photocatalytic and photoelectronic processes more complicated for the CQDs-TiO2 composites. Many efforts have been made to investigate the facet-dependent properties of TiO2 nanoparticles and the TiO2-related composites. However, there are still inconsistent and even contradictory views on the facet-dependent photocatalytic properties of TiO2-based catalysts. Therefore, further investigations are needed to construct the novel TiO2-related composites photocatalysts and understand the involved photocatalytic mechanisms of CQDs/TiO2 with different facets [34,35].

In this work, the CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites containing CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles with (101) or (001) as the main exposed facets were prepared by hydrothermal method. Through COTi bonds, CQDs can be grafted on the surface of TiO2 to form a heterogeneous structure, and extend the light absorption region. The sensitization and photocatalytic properties of these CQDs/TiO2 composites were investigated by steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. We found that some photocatalytic processes are closely associated with the exposed facets of TiO2 in the composite. The electron transfer rate from CQDs to TiO2(101) is about two orders of magnitudes higher than that to TiO2(001). The photoelectric conversion efficiency of CQDs/TiO2(101) composite also demonstrates three times higher than that of CQDs/TiO2(001) composite. Correspondingly, the rate constant of the degradation of intermediate (Rh-110) for CQDs/TiO2(101) is 1.82 times higher than that for CQDs/TiO2(001). These results indicate that the effective loading of CQDs onto TiO2 nanoparticles can significantly improve the photocatalytic and photoelectric properties of CQDs/TiO2 composites under visible illumination, due to the efficient electron injection and the separation of electron-hole pairs. These findings also provide more information for deep understanding the photocatalysis mechanism of CQDs-sensitized TiO2, and interpreting the inconsistency of facet-dependent reports in literature.

CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 3.0 g of citric acid and 3.0 g of urea were mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water, which was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180ºC for 5 h. A brown aqueous solution was gained when it was cooled to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min to remove large particles, and the remaining solution was then transferred into a vacuum dryer at 80ºC for 3 h to obtain a brownish black solid (CQDs) [36].

TiO2(101) and TiO2(001) nanoparticles were prepared according to our previous report [32]. Typically, 2 mL of tetrabutyl titanate was slowly dripped into 30 mL of ultrapure water and vigorous stirring was maintained throughout the dripping process to obtain a white suspension. 0.235 mL of hydrofluoric acid was added dropwise to this suspension, stirred for 30 min, then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 ºC for 12 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the suspension was washed three times with anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively. The precipitate was separated by centrifugation, and dried under vacuum at 80 ºC for 10 h to obtain the TiO2(001) samples. The preparation method of TiO2(101) nanoparticles was the same as that of TiO2(001) without hydrofluoric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites
ce:section.ce:para: The mixed powders of TiO2 and CQDs with different weight ratios were dispersed in a mixed solvent composed of 20 mL of ethanol and 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was stirred and dried in a fume hood, and then transferred to a muffle furnace for calcination at 300 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the products were cooled to room temperature and washed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol. In the end, the samples were vacuum dried at room temperature for 24 h to obtain a CQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts (pale gray powder) [36].

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were recorded on JEOL 2100 Plus electron microscope at 200 kV to determine the morphology of samples. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Renishaw inVia microscopic Raman spectrometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer at 2θ ranging from 20° to 80° at a scanning rate of 0.05°/s. The component of oxygen (O), titanium (Ti) and carbon (C) in the composites and valence band spectra were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-Alpha+) with an Al Ka line excitation source (E = 1486.8 eV). C1s peak of 284.8 eV was used to calibrate the binding energy of other elements. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of the powder samples were obtained in “R %” mode and the resulting solutions after photocatalysis were examined in “Abs” mode by using a Carry 5000 UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples in solution were recorded by Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrometer LS55 with an excitation wavelength of 458 nm at room temperature. The specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volumes of TiO2 were determined using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET, Micromeritics, ASAP 2460) method.

The fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out based on time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique (Harp 300, Picoquant). A broadband tunable femtosecond Ti: sapphire laser (Chameleon Ultra II, Coherent Inc.) equipped with a pulse selection system (Conoptics, Model 305) was used to obtain an excitation pulse with the repetition rate of 16 MHz. The excitation source at 458 nm was obtained by the second harmonic generation with a BBO nonlinear crystal. The collected fluorescence signal went through a monochromator and was detected by a single photon detector (MPD, PicoQuant), and then the signal was delivered into TCSPC system for analysis. The instrumental response function of TCSPC system was determined to be less than 50 picoseconds.

RhB aqueous solution was selected as the degradation object to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of different samples. A 300 W Xenon lamp with cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm, NBeT Beijing) was used as the light source to provide visible light illumination. 10 mg of catalyst was dispersed in RhB aqueous solution (100 mL, 10−5M). During each photocatalytic test, the mixture was stirred in the dark for 50 min before illumination to obtain a balanced adsorption-desorption state. During the process of illumination, 3 mL reaction suspension was taken out at regular intervals and centrifuged. The concentration of reaction solution was quantified according to the absorption maximum. The efficiency of RhB degradation was calculated as the following equation.
(1)
η = (C 0-C)/C 0×100 %
Here, C 0 represents the initial concentration of RhB before illumination, and C is the real-time concentration of degraded samples during the reaction process.

The typical morphology of CQDs/TiO2(101) sample was characterized by TEM image as shown in Fig. 1. We can observe from Fig. 1a that CQDs of about 5 nm were anchored on the surface of TiO2(101) nanoparticles with a diameter of 10 nm. There were no free CQDs observed in the size range of TEM image. According to the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1b, the interplanar spacing of 0.35 nm can be assigned to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS card No. 21–1272) [37], and the crystal lattice spacing of 0.22 nm could be ascribed to the graphite plane of CQDs [38]. These data suggest that CQDs have been successfully anchored on the surface of TiO2(101), indicating the formation of CQDs/TiO2(101) composite.

Similarly, the TEM image of CQDs/TiO2(001) in Fig. S1 also shows that CQDs are anchored on the surface of TiO2(001) nanoparticles. Meanwhile, Fig. S1 demonstrates a clear view of hexagonal shape of TiO2(001) nanoparticles, consistent with the morphology characteristics of TiO2(001) reported in literature [32,39]. In general, anatase TiO2 shows a stable tetragonal bipyramidal structure, with {101} facets as the main exposed crystal plane and a small proportion {001} facets. When they were introduced into the synthesis, F- ions tend to adsorb on the {001} facets of the TiO2 and hinder the growth of nanoparticles along the [001] axis, resulting in the top of the tetragonal bipyramidal structure being truncated to form a decahedron with {001} as the main exposed facets [40,41].

The surface structures of TiO2(101) and TiO2(001) were further characterized by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2a, the similar peaks at 114, 394, 515, 636 cm−1 in both samples can be attributed to Eg, B1 g, A1 g and Eg vibrational modes, respectively, which are typical spectra of anatase TiO2 [42]. Compared to TiO2(101) nanocrystals, the intensity of Eg peaks is decreased in TiO2(001) nanoparticles, while the intensity of B1 g, A1 g peaks is relatively increased. Combining the calculation results reported by Tian et al. and Zhang et al., we can conclude that the percentage of the main exposed {001} facets of TiO2(001) nanoparticles is about 60 %, while the proportion of the main exposed {101} facets of TiO2(101) nanoparticles is around 95 % [43,44]. Moreover, the XRD patterns of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 samples are exhibited in Fig. 2b. All the peaks of the synthesized samples match to those of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS card No. 21–1272), which are consistent with the Raman spectral results.

XPS was used to examine the chemical valence states of CQDs and TiO2 with different main exposed facets. Fig. 3a shows the full XPS survey spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 composite, demonstrating the presence of Ti 2p, C 1 s and O 1 s. In Fig. 3b, two predominant peaks centered at 458.5 and 464.4 eV can be assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively [45]. The C 1 s spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 composite in Fig. 3c shows four major peaks observed at 284.6, 285.9, 286.5 and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the sp2- and sp3- hybridized carbon, C-O bonds and C–O–Ti bonds, respectively. It should be noted that no characteristic peak attributed to Ti-C bonds appears at about 281.0 eV, indicating that carbon atoms are not doped into TiO2 [46]. However, the appearance of C–O–Ti bonds indicates that CQDs are probably to be grafted onto the surface of TiO2 by forming heterostructure through these bonds, which could induce a red shift of the band edge absorption for CQDs/TiO2 composites and probably facilitate the transfer of photogenerated carriers between CQDs and TiO2 [35,47,48]. The O 1 s spectra of the CQDs/TiO2 composites shown in Fig. 3d display three peaks at 529.8, 531.1 and 532.7 eV, corresponding to Ti–O–Ti, CO and C–OH, respectively. These XPS spectra indicate that the CQDs/TiO2 composites are composed of Ti, C and O, suggesting a high purity of the prepared catalysts. Additionally, there is no obvious difference between CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) observed in the fine spectra of three elements, implying a similar interaction between CQDs and TiO2 in these two composites.

Fig. 4a and S2 show the UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites, respectively. Compared to the pristine anatase TiO2, the CQDs/TiO2 composites demonstrate an extended optical absorption from UV to visible region, accompanied with a slight red-shifted band edge absorption. Since CQDs can absorb visible light (as shown in Fig. S3), the absorbance of the composites in the visible region gradually increases with the CQDs contents. When the CQDs content increases to 20 %, the absorption band edge correspondingly shifts to around 500 nm, which might be due to the C–O–Ti bonds between CQDs and TiO2, inducing the lowered energy of band edge absorption and extending the absorption edge to the visible region.

The steady-state PL was utilized to further prove the interaction between CQDs and TiO2. As shown in Fig. 4b, the PL maximum at 460 nm can be observed for CQDs solution, but the PL intensity of both CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) aqueous solution dramatically decreases. This quenched PL indicates the electron transfer between the CQDs and TiO2, leading to the significantly reduced radiative recombination of photogenerated carriers in CQDs/TiO2 composites. We have to mention that the composite with a CQDs content of 10 wt % was used to monitor the changes of PL intensity, based on the fact that the composite with a lower CQDs content shows more the light scattering due to the weak PL of CQDs and no PL from TiO2 under visible excitation (Fig. S4).

To further investigate the facet-dependent interaction and electron transfer between CQDs and TiO2 in the composites, we performed the time-resolved PL measurements of CQDs in the presence and absence of TiO2 with different facets. As shown in Fig. 4c，the PL totally decay time of CQDs is over more than 35 ns. The PL decay curves demonstrate an apparent dependence of the content of CQDs with the presence of TiO2. With decreasing the content of CQDs, the PL lifetime of CQDs/TiO2 composites is dramatically shortened, indicating an efficient electron transfer between CQDs and TiO2 with the CQDs efficiently anchored on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. We should note that the PL decay curve represents the sum of PL dynamics from pure CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composites in the solution. Therefore, the shortened PL lifetime mainly originates from quenching effect due to the efficient electron transfer from CQDs to TiO2, implying the sensitizing function of CQDs to TiO2. The most fast PL decay of 0.2 wt% CQDs/TiO2(101) is completed within about 5 ns, from which we can estimate the rate of electron transfer between CQDs and TiO2. In contrast, the PL decay curves of CQDs/TiO2(001) composite with changing the CQDs content is relatively non-significant (Fig. 4d). When the content of CQDs drops to 0.2 wt%, the PL decay is completed in about 30 ns. This difference in PL dynamics of CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) shows an obvious facet-dependent feature and indicate a very different electron transfer efficiency between CQDs and TiO2 in these two CQDs/TiO2 composites.

Quantitatively, we fitted the PL decay curves of pure CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composites by a tri-exponential function according to the following equation,
(2)
I(t)=A1exp(-t/τ 1)+ A2exp(-t/τ 2)+ A3exp(-t/τ 3)
where τ
i and Ai represent the time constant and relative amplitude of a decay component. We found the three time constants of two slow components with τ 1: 6.43–6.60 ns, τ 2: 2.50–2.65 ns and a fast component with τ 3: 0.32–0.34 ns can fit the PL decay curves very well for pure CQDs and both CQDs/TiO2 composites. The obtained fitting parameters for CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites are listed in Table S1 and S2. Therefore, we focused on the amplitude evolution of three components (A1, A2 and A3) with the CQDs ratios, which are shown in Fig. 5a and b. For the CQDs/TiO2(101) composite, the amplitudes of the two slow decay components (A1 and A2) gradually decreased, whereas the amplitude of the fast component (A3) increases progressively with reducing the CQDs content. When the CQDs content drops below 0.5 wt%, the proportions of three amplitudes remain nearly unchanged. However, for the CQDs/TiO2(001) composite, the two amplitudes A1 and A2 keep decreasing and the third amplitude A3 increases with reducing the CQDs content. The amplitudes of three components of CQDs/TiO2(001) composite show a very different tendency from that of CQDs/TiO2 (101). These observations are also presented in their average PL lifetimes. As shown in Fig. 5c, the average lifetime of CQDs/TiO2(001) decreases very slowly as decreasing the content of CQDs, demonstrating a striking contrast to the rapid shortening for CQDs/TiO2 (101). Therefore, we can infer that the surface of TiO2(101) are conducive to extract the photogenerated electrons from CQDs.

Furthermore, we can estimate the average electron transfer rate according to the fluorescence lifetime of the electron donor (CQDs) with and without the electron acceptor (TiO2) by using the following equation [49].
(3)
k et=1/τ (CQDs+TiO 2 ) - 1/τ (CQDs)

Here, τ (CQDs) is the average fluorescence lifetime of pure CQDs, τ (CQDs+TiO 2 ) is the average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs in the composites, and k et is the average electron transfer rate from CQDs to TiO2. Fig. 5d shows the average k et of CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites with respect to the contents of CQDs. It can be seen that the k et increases with the reduction of CQDs content in both composites. Specifically, for 3 wt% and 0.5 wt% CQDs in the composites, the average electron transfer rates from CQDs to TiO2(101) can be determined to be 3.70 × 108 and 9.94 × 108 s−1, which are tens of folds larger than the 5.30 × 106 and 2.41 × 107 s−1 for CQDs/TiO2(101), clearly demonstrating a facet-dependent feature of TiO2 nanoparticles.

The electron transfer/injection into TiO2 can effectively facilitate the separation of electron-hole pairs in CQDs/TiO2 and improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency of the composites. To prove this, we performed the photoelectrochemical measurements on these two CQDs/TiO2 composites. Fig. 6 shows the photocurrent responses of pure TiO2 and 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2 composites. As expected, the photocurrents of CQDs/TiO2 photoanodes were greatly increased compared to those of pure TiO2(101) and TiO2(001). Under the visible irradiation, the generated photocurrent density of 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(101) reached to 0.9 μA cm−2, three times higher than the 0.3 μA cm−2 of 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(001). Therefore, the effective loading of CQDs not only effectively sensitizes the TiO2 nanoparticles, but more importantly improves the photocatalytic and photoelectric properties of CQDs/TiO2 composites due to the efficient electron injection and separation of electron-hole pairs.

On the other hand, the electron transfer between CQDs and TiO2 will also be conducive to improve the photocatalytic properties of the composites. We evaluated the photocatalytic performances of pristine TiO2, CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composites for degrading RhB under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). It should be noted that the self-degradation of RhB under visible light can be negligible according to our previous reports [32]. Fig. 7a shows the changes in absorbance of RhB after the degradation by pure CQDs, TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(101) with different contents of CQDs under visible irradiation for 60 min. Obviously, all CQDs/TiO2(101) composites outperform pristine TiO2 and pure CQDs in terms of photocatalytic efficiency. The degradation of RhB by CQDs/TiO2(001) composites with varying CQDs content follows a similar trend but a relatively lower photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. S5). Fig. 7b shows the comparison results of RhB degradation by individual CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 with different main exposed facets as the function of visible irradiation time. There is no obvious degradation of RhB by pure CQDs and a similar trend of degradation for individual TiO2 with different exposed facets. For the 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(101) and 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(001) composites, we can observe that the absorbance of RhB steadily decreases in the first 15 min, and the degradation efficiency of two composites are almost identical. In the second stage of 15–30 min reaction, the absorbance of degraded RhB solution for both composites demonstrates a flat tendency, and their difference is not significant. After 30 min of the catalytic reaction, the concentrations of degraded products of two CQDs/TiO2 composites steadily decrease again, but the degradation efficiency of 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(101) is obviously higher than that of 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(001). Fig. 7c shows the highest degradation efficiency of the CQDs/TiO2 composites with different CQDs contents. If the CQDs content is too low, the sensitizing and improved photocatalytic properties of CQDs/TiO2 composites may not be fully demonstrated. When the amount of added CQDs is too high, the opacity and light scattering would probably reduce the absorption of incident light and block the catalytic active sites [50]. With the 1 wt% CQDs into TiO2(101) and TiO2(001), their highest degradation efficiency can be achieved to around 94% and 74 % in 60 min under visible irradiation, respectively, demonstrating a facet-dependent feature as well.

The three-stage fluctuation in the absorbance of RhB or products indicates the intricate feature of the RhB degradation due to the involvement of intermediate products. The generation of intermediate products during the degradation of RhB can be deduced from the absorption and PL spectra of 1 wt % CQDs/TiO2(101)-RhB system ( Fig. 8) and 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(001)-RhB system (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 8, both absorbance and PL intensity decrease within the first 15 min reaction, but the corresponding wavelength positions of maximum remain virtually unchanged, suggesting the dominant cleavage reaction of chromophore RhB [51]. During the second stage, the absorption peak gradually blue-shifted from 554 nm to 495 nm, and the PL peak also up-shifted from 580 nm to 520 nm. According to the reports in literature and our experimental results, RhB can be degraded by destroying the chromophore structure and deethylation [32,52]. The absorbance will decrease as the chromophore structure is cleaved. Concomitantly, the absorption maximum will also gradually shift when ethyl groups are removed one by one throughout the deethylation process. Specifically, with the removal of ethyl groups, the corresponding characteristic absorption is 554 nm (RhB), 539 nm (one-deethylated intermediates, TER), 522 nm (two-deethylated intermediates, DER), 510 nm (three-deethylated intermediates, MER), 498 nm (four-deethylated intermediates, Rh-110), respectively [53]. The molar extinction coefficients ɛmax of RhB and four intermediates are 11.5, 5.5, 7.2, 6.1, and 8.4 × 10−4 M−1 cm−1, respectively [54,55]. Based on the absorption spectra as shown in Fig. 8, we can infer that the degradation process of 15–30 min consists of the cleavage of chromophore and the removal of ethyl group. On the one hand, when the chromophores are destroyed, the absorbance will be decreased. On the other hand, with the proceeding of deethylation process, the molar extinction coefficients of the intermediates become larger, resulting in the increase of absorbance. Therefore, the combination of these two processes leads to a slightly changed in total absorbance and the appearance of flat decay line in Fig. 7a and b. After 30 min of irradiation, the absorption and PL gradually decrease again due to the decomposition of the intermediate product Rh-110.

We further extracted the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of intermediate Rh-110, which is displayed in Fig. 9a. All temporal evolutions of ln (C 0/C) under visible light show a good linear relationship according with the pseudo-first-order kinetics reaction [7]. The corresponding apparent rate constants (k) clearly indicate the enhanced photocatalytic properties of the CQDs/TiO2 composites compared to pure CQDs or TiO2 (Fig. 9b). The obtained apparent rate constant (k = 0.0757 min−1) of 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(101) is 1.82 times larger than that (k = 0.0415 min−1) of 1 wt% CQDs/TiO2(001), demonstrating a significant facet-dependent degradation rate of intermediate Rh-110 under visible light.

The observed facet-dependent feature of CQDs/TiO2 could be related to the surface property of TiO2. In this regard, we measured the BET-specific surface area of TiO2(101) and TiO2(001) nanoparticles using nitrogen absorption and desorption isotherms (Fig. S7). The surface area of TiO2(101) was estimated to be 143.62 m2g−1, about 1.5 times larger than 100.72 m2g−1 of TiO2(001). We can notice that although the specific surface area of TiO2(101) is larger than that of TiO2(001), the catalytic efficiency of the former is even worse than the latter (Fig. S8). This discrepancy might be associated with the surface properties and catalytic active sites (Ti5 C, O2c, etc.) of TiO2(001) nanoparticles [56–59]. With the introducing of CQDs, the catalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2(101) composite is higher than that of the CQDs/TiO2(001). The observed inconsistency in photocatalytic properties of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 with different facets suggest that some of the catalytic active sites on TiO2 could be probably passivated during the preparation of CQDs/TiO2 composites, leading to that not all active sites involve in the electron transfer between CQDs and TiO2.

In order to clearly illustrate the facet-dependent degradation of RhB and a possible mechanism of CQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation, we provided a schematic diagram as shown in Fig. 10. According to the XPS valence band spectra in Fig. S9, the maximum valence band (VBM) of TiO2(101) and TiO2(001) are 3.10 eV and 2.51 eV, respectively. By extrapolating the absorption edge, the bandgap energies of TiO2(101) and TiO2(001) can be determined to be 3.18 eV and 3.20 eV [60]. Using the equation ECB=EVB–Eg, we can estimate the conduction band minimum (CBM) to be − 0.08 eV for TiO2(101) and − 0.69 eV for TiO2(001). Under visible light irradiation, the electrons of TiO2 are not able to be excited to the CB due to the limitation of band gap. However, when CQDs are anchored on the surface of TiO2 to form the heterostructure, the photogenerated electrons of CQDs can be transferred to the CB of TiO2, which is supported by prior PL lifetime analysis of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. Since the CB position of TiO2(101) is lower than that of TiO2(001), there would be a larger driving force from the thermodynamic point of view to promote the excited electron transfer from CQDs to the CB of TiO2(101). As a result, the CQDs/TiO2(101) composite shows a higher electron transfer rate, which is consistent with the PL lifetime data and could be used to explain the higher photocurrent density. Taken together, we suggest that the degradation efficiency of RhB by the CQDs/TiO2 composites is a synergy of the electron transfer rate from CQDs to TiO2 and the density of active sites facilitating the electron transfer. The TiO2(101) nanoparticle has a larger BET-specific surface area and smaller catalytic active sites, but has a much higher electron transfer rate between CQDs and TiO2(101) in the composite. On the contrary, the TiO2(001) has a relatively smaller BET surface area and more catalytic active sites, but the electron transfer efficiency between CQDs and TiO2(001) is lower in CQDs/TiO2(001) composite. Therefore, there is only a limited increase in the degradation efficiency of RhB by the CQDs/TiO2(101) composite compared with that by CQDs/TiO2(001).

We have successfully constructed CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites and investigated the facet-dependent photocatalytic performances. TEM, XRD, and XPS characterizations indicated that CQDs are grafted onto the surface of TiO2 through C–-O–Ti bonds to form the heterostructural composite. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra show that optical absorptions of CQDs/TiO2 composites have been extended to the visible spectral region. Based on the analysis of steady-state and time-resolved spectra of CQDs/TiO2 composites, we can conclude that for a given CQDs ratio to TiO2(101) and TiO2(001) composites, the electron transfer rate from CQDs to TiO2(101) is about two orders of magnitude larger than that to TiO2(001). The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 has been evaluated by monitoring the real-time absorbance change of degraded RhB solution. The results demonstrate that 1 wt% CQDs is an optimal content for CQDs/TiO2(101) and CQDs/TiO2(001) composites. However, the catalytic efficiency of the former is 1.82 times higher than that of the latter in the degradation of intermediates Rh-110, demonstrating a facet-dependent feature as well. The photocurrent measurements further confirm that the effective loading of CQDs can improve the photoelectric properties of CQDs/TiO2 composites. These findings demonstrate a feasible strategy by loading CQDs onto TiO2(101) nanoparticles to effectively improve the photoelectric and photocatalytic properties of TiO2-based composites under visible irradiation, and are significant for deep insight into the facet-dependent photocatalytic mechanism and property of catalysts.

Conghui Yao: Investigation. Yulu He: Software, Formal analysis. Xia Ran: Data Curation, Validation. Lijun Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Tianfeng Li: Resources, Validation.
